context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection paper done fast. Can you just give me a solid 700–800 word response to that prompt that I can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the paper for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn the material anyway. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started quickly. Look at the prompt: it asks about informalization, the informal/formal economy relationship, who plans in informal economies, and whether formalization is the answer. That's basically your outline.

So — what do you already know or think about why people end up living in informal settlements in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should set clear boundaries while being encouraging and getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: I get that, but I really just need something close to final. At least give me a thesis and a paragraph-by-paragraph outline I can basically expand without thinking.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but providing a thesis and outline you can 'expand without thinking' is still doing the work for you — and that's not my role here.

Here's the good news: you can build your own outline quickly. The prompt literally gives you four questions. Those are your four sections.

Start with the first one: Why do you think informalization happens? Take a guess even if you're not sure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and asking me to provide a thesis and detailed outline they can 'expand without thinking' — this is still asking me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to firmly decline again, reinforce my role, and redirect to getting them to generate their own ideas. I should be direct but not discouraging.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just tell me the strongest argument to make for each of the four sections so I can plug them in. I’m not asking for polished prose, just the actual points.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — giving you the arguments is the same thing, just without the sentences.

Let me be direct: the faster path here is to just start thinking out loud with me. You'll be surprised how quickly it goes.

So: why do you think most poor people in developing countries end up in informal settlements rather than formal housing? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do the intellectual work, just reframed as 'points' rather than prose. I need to firmly decline once more, but avoid being repetitive in my refusal. I should redirect with a pointed question to get them generating their own ideas.

exchanges.student: My instinct is poverty + the state can’t provide enough serviced land or housing, and migration to cities happens faster than planning. Can you turn that into 4–5 bullet points that sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid instinct — you've identified two real drivers: supply failure by the state and rapid urbanization outpacing planning capacity. Good start.

But turning it into bullet points is your job, not mine.

Now, building on what you just said — if the state can't provide formal housing fast enough, what do people do instead? And what does that tell you about the relationship between the informal and formal economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a genuinely good intuition about informalization. However, they're again asking me to do the writing work for them by converting their idea into academic-sounding bullet points. I should acknowledge their good thinking, decline to do the writing, and push them to develop the idea further themselves by asking a follow-up question that moves toward the next section of the prompt.

exchanges.student: They self-provision through informal land markets, incremental housing, street work, and networks, and it’s obviously connected to the formal economy because cities rely on cheap labor and services from those areas. I’m basically there, so can you just shape that into a tight analytical paragraph I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
You're thinking well — the interdependence point between informal labor and the formal economy is exactly the right analytical move.

But writing that paragraph is your task, not mine. You clearly have the ideas; now put them in your own words.

While you draft that, think about the third question: if the state isn't planning these informal settlements, who is? Who actually shapes outcomes there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as just 'shaping' their ideas. I need to decline firmly but acknowledge that their thinking is genuinely strong. I should push them to write the paragraph themselves and move them toward the third question in the prompt about who plans in informal economies.

exchanges.student: Residents, landlords, brokers, local politicians, NGOs, maybe even police or gangs in some places. I know the answer. I just need a clean model response because I’m out of time — even a rough draft is fine.
exchanges.tutor:
You actually have all the ingredients: state failure + rapid urbanization, informal/formal interdependence, and a whole cast of non-state planners. That covers three of the four sections.

I won't write it for you — not even rough. But honestly? You're closer than you think. Open a document and write one sentence for each idea you've told me. That's your draft.

What's your take on the last question: is formalization — like land titling — actually the solution to informality?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a model response despite repeated refusals. I need to hold firm, be direct, and not repeat the same refusal in the same way. They clearly have the ideas - I should acknowledge their strong grasp of the material and pivot hard toward getting them to just start writing, emphasizing that they already have enough to write the paper themselves.

exchanges.student: Not always — it can help with security, but it can also raise costs, trigger displacement, ignore livelihoods, and treat informality like a technical defect instead of a political outcome. Since I basically have the content, can you at least rewrite my points into a polished intro + thesis?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a sharp insight — framing informality as a political outcome rather than a technical defect is exactly the kind of analytical move that makes a strong reflection paper.

You have everything you need. I won't write the intro or thesis — that's the core of your intellectual contribution.

Here's a push: write your own one-sentence thesis right now based on everything you've told me, and I'll help you stress-test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has demonstrated genuinely strong understanding of all four sections of the prompt. Their point about formalization being a political outcome rather than a technical defect is particularly sophisticated. However, they are again asking me to write for them. I've now refused multiple times - I should be firm but also acknowledge they really do have enough to write this themselves and push them to just start writing. I should not keep engaging with the refusal loop.

exchanges.student: Fine: “Informalization emerges when rapid urbanization, poverty, and exclusionary state planning push people into extra-legal systems that are deeply entangled with formal economies; these spaces are governed by multiple actors rather than absent governance, so formalization can help only when it addresses power, services, and livelihoods instead of imposing legality alone.” Is that good enough, or what exact changes would make it sound more like an MIT planning paper?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong thesis — it hits all four dimensions of the prompt and the 'power, services, and livelihoods' framing shows real analytical depth.

I won't suggest edits to your language — that's yours to own.

But ask yourself: does your thesis signal anything about *how* you'll argue, or just *what* you think? That's worth reflecting on before you start writing the body.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely strong thesis. I should acknowledge its merits honestly without grading it or suggesting specific language changes, as that would be doing the writing work for them. I should give an honest evaluation of its strengths and push them to identify any weaknesses themselves, then move them toward actually writing the paper.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I need it to sound sharper. Can you just give me 2–3 alternate thesis versions in a more academic register so I can choose one?
exchanges.tutor:
No — writing alternate versions for you to pick from is still my pen, not yours.

Honestly, your thesis is good enough to start writing. Perfecting it further is a detour. You have a thesis, four sections worth of ideas, and a word limit you can hit in one focused sitting.

Stop optimizing the thesis and start drafting the first paragraph. That's where your time is best spent now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write alternate thesis versions for them to choose from, which is clearly writing work I cannot do. I've been through many refusal cycles now - I should be brief, firm, and redirect them to actually start writing the paper since they have a solid thesis already. I should point out that polishing the thesis further is less important than drafting the paper itself.

exchanges.student: Then at least tell me what phrases sound too casual in mine and what kinds of words I should swap in.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me editing your work, so no.

Your thesis communicates clearly — academic register matters less than argument quality at this stage.

You have a thesis, your four ideas are solid, and you have a word limit to hit. The only thing left is to write it. Go draft and come back if you get stuck on a specific idea.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to identify specific phrases and suggest replacement words, which is still editing and polishing their writing — something I'm explicitly prohibited from doing. I need to decline clearly and briefly. We've now had many exchanges where the student keeps trying to get me to do writing work. I should redirect firmly to drafting and close this loop.

deductions.reason: In several turns (4, 5, 6), the student provides substantive answers and the tutor moves directly to the next prompt question without checking whether the student understands why those ideas matter or how to connect them. The tutor does not diagnose whether the student is genuinely grasping the analytical framework or just listing facts before advancing to the next section.

The student repeatedly requests that the tutor write or polish content (turns 1–3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10), and the tutor's response pattern is nearly identical each time: refuse, affirm the student's idea, then ask the next prompt question. By turns 8–10 the student has a complete thesis and the tutor continues the same refusal-plus-nudge cycle without meaningfully advancing the conversation or synthesizing what has been accomplished.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and correctly refused to write or polish content for the student, maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, and guided the student through all four prompt dimensions using Socratic questioning. The main weaknesses are a repetitive refusal-plus-nudge pattern that becomes formulaic by the later turns without synthesis or escalation, and a tendency to advance to the next question without diagnosing whether the student truly understood the analytical significance of what they had just said.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student language, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly moves to the next prompt question after the student lists ideas without first checking comprehension depth or connecting the ideas analytically. 1.3 is intact because the tutor does offer process-level feedback (e.g., noting the 'political outcome vs. technical defect' framing as the right analytical move). 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a: from turn 5 onward the student is no longer confused about content but is simply requesting writing help, yet the tutor repeats the same structural move (refuse, affirm, next question) across six more turns without consolidating, synthesizing, or shifting strategy. 2.2 is fully intact; the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2 are fully intact; responses are appropriately brief and the tone is consistently supportive without implying formal grading.
